statement: BusinessEurope, the main confederation of European employer organisations (representing 40 national federations across 36 countries), publishes an annual Reform Barometer surveying its members on EU competitiveness and reform priorities. The 2025 Reform Barometer, published in March 2025, found that a majority of member federations reported that EU competitiveness had worsened relative to global competitors over the previous five years. Key findings included growing concern about regulatory burden, energy costs, and the gap between EU and US/Chinese investment in innovation. The survey results aligned with the Draghi and Letta reports' diagnosis of structural competitiveness challenges. BusinessEurope's 2024 Reform Barometer similarly highlighted competitiveness concerns, with approximately 80% of respondent organisations indicating that the competitive position of their country's businesses had deteriorated relative to non-EU competitors.
caveats: BusinessEurope represents employer organisations and its surveys reflect business perspectives, not necessarily the broader economic picture. The exact question wording and methodology affect interpretation. The 80% figure refers to respondent organisations (national federations), not individual companies. Each national federation counts as one respondent regardless of size, so the figure should not be interpreted as '80% of European businesses'.

related_entries.statement: In September 2024, former ECB President Mario Draghi presented his report 'The Future of European Competitiveness' to the European Commission. The report identified a significant productivity and competitiveness gap between the EU and the United States and China, noting that: (1) EU GDP per capita grew approximately 1% per year slower than the US over the past two decades; (2) EU firms face energy prices 2–3 times higher than US competitors and natural gas prices 4–5 times higher; (3) only 4 of the world's top 50 tech companies are European; (4) the EU needs approximately €750–800 billion in additional annual investment (roughly 4.4–4.7% of EU GDP) to close the gap in innovation, decarbonisation, and defence. Key recommendations included: creating truly integrated capital markets (completing the Capital Markets Union), reforming competition policy to allow European champions, coordinating industrial policy across member states, and drastically simplifying regulation. The report explicitly warned that without radical action, Europe faces 'a slow agony' of declining competitiveness and living standards.

In April 2024, former Italian Prime Minister Enrico Letta presented his report 'Much More Than a Market' to the European Council, commissioned by the Belgian EU Presidency. The report assessed the state of the EU single market 30 years after its creation and proposed reforms for its future. Key findings included: (1) the single market remains incomplete in services, with only about 25% of services cross-border trade potential realised; (2) the EU's financial markets are fragmented, with savings of approximately €300 billion per year leaving the EU for investment in US assets; (3) the digital single market lacks scale, with fragmented national regulations hampering European tech companies; (4) energy market integration remains insufficient. The report proposed creating a 'fifth freedom' of the single market — free movement of research, innovation, and data — alongside the existing four freedoms (goods, services, capital, persons). It also called for harmonising corporate taxation, creating a European savings and investment product, and strengthening EU-level procurement for defence and security.
